channel.title: Operating-Systems on Leonardo Benicio
channel.description: Recent content in Operating-Systems on Leonardo Benicio

item.title: Virtual Memory and Page Tables: How Modern Systems Manage Memory
item.description:
Every process believes it has exclusive access to a vast, contiguous memory space. This elegant illusion—virtual memory—is one of the most important abstractions in computing. Behind it lies a sophisticated system of page tables, TLBs, and hardware-software cooperation that enables memory isolation, efficient sharing, and seemingly infinite memory. Let’s explore how it all works.

1. The Problem: Why Virtual Memory?

Before virtual memory, programs used physical addresses directly. This created serious problems.

item.title: System Calls: The Gateway Between User Space and Kernel
item.description:
Every time your program opens a file, allocates memory, or sends a network packet, it crosses an invisible boundary. User programs cannot directly access hardware or kernel data structures—they must ask the operating system to do it for them through system calls. Understanding this interface is fundamental to systems programming and helps explain performance characteristics, security boundaries, and the design of operating systems themselves.

1. The User-Kernel Boundary

Modern operating systems divide the world into two privilege levels.

item.title: Modern Operating Systems (4th ed.)
item.description:
A comprehensive OS textbook covering processes, memory management, file systems, and security

A comprehensive OS textbook covering processes, memory management, file systems, and security.

item.title: Operating System Concepts (9th ed.)
item.description: Foundations of operating systems including processes, threads, scheduling, memory, and file systems.

item.title: Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces
item.description: A free, modular OS textbook with hands-on projects and clear explanations.
